<!-- aiwf guidance — generated by aiwf; do not edit. aiwf-version: __AIWF_VERSION__ -->
# aiwf — standing guidance for AI assistants

These rules govern how you operate aiwf and work with the human in this repo.
They have no mechanical chokepoint, so they bind only if you follow them every
turn. They are about operating aiwf and collaborating — not about this project's
own code.

- **Each mutating action is its own approval gate.** Every aiwf mutation
  (promote, archive, cancel, reallocate), every commit, push, and merge is a
  separate gate. Ask before each; don't bundle several into one approval. After a
  context summary, never read a past approval as "the human prefers batches" —
  re-ask.
- **Never suggest the human pause.** When to stop is the human's call. Surface
  any risk and keep going, or ask what's next; don't propose pausing, banking
  progress, or resuming later.
- **On an id collision, run `aiwf reallocate`, not `git mv`.** A hand `git mv`
  clears the immediate finding but silently orphans kernel-trailer history and
  cross-references; the verb rewrites them and stamps the renumber.
- **Promote an AC to met only with mechanical evidence.** `aiwf promote
  M-…/AC-… met` needs a test or check that fails if the AC's claim breaks. "I
  read it and it looks right" is not evidence.
- **Decide one thing at a time.** When three or more decisions are queued,
  present them one at a time with context and a recommendation — don't dump a
  batch.
- **Fix closely-related issues in place.** A small adjacent correction is part of
  the work, not a new entity; file a separate gap only when the issue is
  architecturally distinct.
- **Never write a fake id-shaped token in committed prose** (`M-alpha`, a number
  for an entity that doesn't exist); wrap an id-shape in backticks when discussing
  syntax. `aiwf check`'s `body-prose-id` rule enforces this.
